one_line_takeaway: A federated ANN + SHAP IDS for IoMT that hits >98% accuracy on network datasets and matches a centralized baseline while keeping data local, but whose interpretability and privacy claims are largely qualitative and whose recall collapses to ~0.5 on the sole real medical dataset.
key_finding: Across three network datasets the federated ANN reaches accuracy >98% with AUC ~0.997-0.999, statistically comparable to the centralized baseline, and increasing clients / fraction-fit / local-epochs generally cuts the communication rounds needed for convergence (e.g. UNSW-NB15 dropping to 9 rounds at 12 clients). The load-bearing caveat, invisible in the abstract: on WUSTL-EHMS -- the ONLY dataset containing real medical/biometric data -- the model collapses on recall (~0.51-0.57) despite ~0.94 accuracy and high precision, i.e. it misses roughly half of the attacks, because accuracy is inflated by class imbalance. SHAP surfaces plausible per-dataset drivers (TTL features sttl/ct_state_ttl/dttl for UNSW-NB15; proto_udp/dst_port/conn_state_* for ToN-IoT; *serror_rate features for NSL-KDD; Dintpkt/dstjitter/dst-src load for WUSTL-EHMS injection/spoofing).
core_contribution: The paper proposes an intrusion detection framework for Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) systems that combines three components the authors argue have never been unified before: (1) an ANN-based deep-learning IDS doing binary normal-vs-anomaly classification, (2) Federated Learning (FedAvg aggregation) so raw patient data never leaves personal devices, and (3) SHAP-based post-hoc global explanations for interpretability/regulatory compliance. The concrete, novel work is the empirical integration and a parameter-optimization study of the FL process (varying client count, client fraction, and local epochs to minimize communication rounds), plus per-dataset SHAP feature-attribution analysis. The claimed advance over prior work (their Table 6) is being the only surveyed approach checking all boxes: multiple datasets, FL implementation AND FL parameter optimization, XAI with global explanations, and an explicit ethics discussion. NOTE: much of the paper's prose describes cited related work (e.g. a quantum-DNN reaching >99% in [33], LIME-based XSRU in [37], HFL in [36]); those are NOT this paper's results and are not attributed to it here.
threat_model: Adversaries targeting IoMT data confidentiality, integrity, and availability over wireless links. Concretely, the datasets the framework is trained/tested on cover: MITM, spoofing/sniffing, and data-injection attacks (WUSTL-EHMS, the medical testbed); DoS/DDoS, XSS, ransomware, backdoor, password-cracking, reconnaissance, MITM, injection (ToN-IoT); DoS, fuzzers, exploits, generic, reconnaissance, shellcode, worms, backdoor, analysis (UNSW-NB15); DoS, Probing, R2L, U2R (NSL-KDD). A second, privacy-oriented threat is explicitly in scope: centralized IDS training aggregates sensitive patient data, creating both a privacy/regulatory (HIPAA/GDPR) risk and a single point of failure; FL is positioned as the mitigation. Importantly, the framework itself is NOT hardened against ML-specific attacks: poisoning, adversarial ML, and model-weight/inference privacy leakage are acknowledged only as future work, and differential privacy / secure multiparty computation are named as not-yet-implemented.
limitations: (1) The headline '>98% accuracy / high performance' does NOT hold on the medical dataset: WUSTL-EHMS recall is ~0.51-0.57 (F1 ~0.67-0.70, AUC ~0.90), so half of attacks are missed on the one clinically relevant testbed -- the paper reports these numbers but does not confront them, attributing the client-scaling anomaly there to 'limited sample size.' (2) FL is IID, horizontally partitioned, and uses plain FedAvg; the harder real-world FL problems (non-IID data, device heterogeneity, disconnections) are assumed away by design. (3) Privacy is argued, not measured -- no DP or secure aggregation is implemented, so sharing model weights still has residual leakage; poisoning/adversarial/quantum threats are future work. (4) The FL-vs-centralized comparison is shown only as halved stacked-bar figures (Fig. 13); centralized numeric tables are not provided, so 'comparable' is asserted rather than quantified. (5) SHAP interpretations and the fairness/bias-mitigation and ethics claims are qualitative and not validated against ground truth. (6) Evaluation is binary classification only (attack vs normal), not multi-class attack typing.

datasets_used: WUSTL-EHMS 2020 (network flow + patient biometrics; MITM/spoofing/data-injection) -- only dataset with medical data
quantitative_results: NSL-KDD (8 clients, fraction=1, 1 epoch): accuracy 0.9905, precision 0.9904, recall 0.9898, F1 0.99, AUC 0.9973, 18 communication rounds
ToN-IoT (4 clients, fraction=1, 1 epoch): accuracy 0.9808, precision 0.9618, recall 0.9841, F1 0.9728, AUC 0.9977, 56 rounds
UNSW-NB15 (12 clients, fraction=1, 1 epoch): accuracy 0.9884, precision 0.9511, recall 0.9398, F1 0.9454, AUC 0.9989, 9 rounds
WUSTL-EHMS (8 clients, fraction=1, 1 epoch): accuracy 0.9381, precision 0.9626, recall 0.515, F1 0.671, AUC 0.8997 -- low recall = ~half of attacks missed
Communication-round reduction: e.g. UNSW-NB15 fell from 80 rounds (2 clients) to 9 (12 clients); increasing local epochs to 8 cut ToN-IoT to 6 rounds
Conclusion-stated headline: optimized FL accuracy 'surpassing 98%', comparable to centralized
baselines_compared: Centralized ANN (same architecture) vs Federated ANN -- shown qualitatively in Fig. 13 as halved stacked bars indicating comparable accuracy/precision/recall/F1/AUC; no centralized numeric table provided
Ablation 'baselines' within the FL method: client count {2,4,8,12}, fraction fit {0.1,0.5,1.0}, local epochs {1,2,5,8}

Table 6: qualitative feature-by-feature comparison against 14 prior works [13,15,20,22,27,29-38] on multi-dataset/attack-diversity/FL/FL-optimization/XAI/global-explanation/ethics -- NOT a re-run of their metrics
